beginner.question: What are the three required sub-properties for a CSS border?
beginner.answer: The three required sub-properties are border-width (thickness, e.g., 2px, thick), border-style (pattern, e.g., solid, dashed, dotted), and border-color (color, e.g., #333, red). However, only border-style is strictly required — the default width is medium and default color is currentColor, but if border-style is omitted (defaults to none), no border appears.

beginner.question: What is the shorthand for setting a border in CSS?
beginner.answer: The border shorthand combines width, style, and color: border: 2px solid #333;. The order is width, style, color. All modern browsers accept them in any order, but the standard convention is width, style, color. Only border-style is required for a visible border.

beginner.question: How do you set borders on individual sides of an element?
beginner.answer: Use the side-specific properties: border-top, border-right, border-bottom, and border-left. Each accepts the same width, style, and color values as the border shorthand. Example: border-top: 1px dashed red; border-bottom: 3px double green; This allows each side to have a different style.

beginner.question: What are the different border-style values available in CSS?
beginner.answer: The border-style values are: solid (single solid line), dashed (dashed line), dotted (dotted line), double (two parallel solid lines), groove (carved-in 3D effect), ridge (raised 3D effect), inset (embedded 3D effect), outset (raised 3D effect), none (no border), and hidden (similar to none but used for table conflict resolution).

beginner.question: What is the default border-style if you only set border-width or border-color?
beginner.answer: The default border-style is none. If you set border-width or border-color without setting border-style, no border will appear on screen because the style defaults to none. This is a common mistake — you must always set border-style for the border to render.

beginner.question: How do you create rounded corners using CSS?
beginner.answer: Use the border-radius property. A single value applies to all corners: border-radius: 10px;. You can set individual corners: border-top-left-radius, border-top-right-radius, border-bottom-right-radius, border-bottom-left-radius. The shorthand accepts 1-4 values (top-left, top-right, bottom-right, bottom-left).

beginner.question: How do you make a perfect circle using border-radius?
beginner.answer: Set border-radius: 50%; on an element with equal width and height. The 50% value applies circular rounding to each corner at half the element's dimension, creating a perfect circle. For example: .circle { width: 100px; height: 100px; border-radius: 50%; background: red; } creates a red circle.

beginner.question: What happens if you set border-radius but not border-style?
beginner.answer: border-radius applies whether or not a border exists. If no border is set, border-radius clips the element's background and content to the rounded shape. This is commonly used to create rounded corners on buttons, cards, and images without needing an actual border: border-radius: 8px; alone creates rounded corners on the element's background.

beginner.question: Does a border add to the total width and height of an element?
beginner.answer: Yes, by default (with box-sizing: content-box), the border width is added to the element's total rendered width and height. An element with width: 200px and border: 5px solid black will be 210px wide total (200 + 5 + 5). With box-sizing: border-box, the border is included within the specified width.

beginner.question: Can a border have a gradient color?
beginner.answer: The border-color property does not directly support gradients. However, you can achieve gradient borders by using border-image with a gradient, or by using a background-gradient on a wrapper element with padding acting as the 'border'. For border-image: border-image: linear-gradient(45deg, red, blue) 1; combined with a transparent border-style.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between the 1-, 2-, 3-, and 4-value shorthands for border-radius?
intermediate.answer: Like margin and padding, border-radius uses the TRBL (top-right-bottom-left) shorthand: 1 value = all corners; 2 values = top-left+bottom-right, top-right+bottom-left; 3 values = top-left, top-right+bottom-left, bottom-right; 4 values = top-left, top-right, bottom-right, bottom-left. Example: border-radius: 10px 20px 30px 40px applies different radii to each corner.

intermediate.question: How does the border-image property work?
intermediate.answer: border-image uses an image slice to create a decorative border. Syntax: border-image: url('border.png') 30 stretch;. The numeric value (30) is the slice size in pixels from each edge of the image. The stretch/round/repeat keyword controls how the middle sections are repeated. border-image requires border-style or border-width to be set, and it overrides the border-color.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between border and outline?
intermediate.answer: Outlines are drawn outside the border edge and do not affect the element's layout dimensions (they overlap margins). Borders are part of the box model and add to dimensions. Outlines cannot be styled per-side — they're the same on all sides. Outlines do not follow border-radius (they stay rectangular). Outlines are primarily used for focus indicators and debugging.

intermediate.question: How do you create a border on only one side while using the shorthand for other sides?
intermediate.answer: Set a default border using the shorthand, then override specific sides: border: 1px solid #ccc; border-bottom: none; removes the bottom border while keeping the others. Or set individual sides: border-top: 2px solid blue; border-left: 1px solid #ddd; border-right: 1px solid #ddd; border-bottom: 1px solid #ddd;

intermediate.question: How does border-collapse: collapse affect borders on table elements?
intermediate.answer: border-collapse: collapse merges adjacent table cell borders into a single border instead of having separate borders with spacing. When collapsed, the border-style with the highest priority wins (hidden > none > solid > double > groove > ridge > inset > outset). Using border-collapse: collapse is common for clean table designs.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between the 'none' and 'hidden' border-style values?
intermediate.answer: Visually, none and hidden both make the border invisible. The difference appears in table border-collapse resolution: hidden has higher priority than none. When two adjacent cells have conflicting border styles, hidden overrides none (and all other styles). This makes hidden useful for selectively removing borders in collapsed tables.

intermediate.question: How can you create a triangle using CSS borders?
intermediate.answer: Create an element with zero width and height, set a transparent border on three sides, and a colored border on the side you want the triangle to point from: .triangle { width: 0; height: 0; border-left: 50px solid transparent; border-right: 50px solid transparent; border-bottom: 50px solid red; } creates an upward-pointing red triangle.

intermediate.question: What is the initial/default value of border-color when not explicitly set?
intermediate.answer: The default value of border-color is currentColor, which is the current value of the element's color property (usually inherited or defaulting to the browser's text color, typically black). This means if you set border: 3px solid without specifying a color, the border will match the element's text color.

intermediate.question: How do border-radius values work with elliptical corners using two values per corner?
intermediate.answer: Border-radius can accept two values per corner separated by a / to create elliptical corners. Example: border-radius: 10px / 20px; creates a 10px horizontal radius and 20px vertical radius. border-radius: 10px 20px 30px 40px / 5px 10px 15px 20px; sets different horizontal and vertical radii for each corner, creating asymmetric, pill-shaped corners.

intermediate.question: How do you create a dashed border with custom dash lengths?
intermediate.answer: CSS does not have a native property to control dash lengths on dashed borders directly. However, you can use the border-image property with a repeating linear-gradient: border: 2px solid transparent; border-image: repeating-linear-gradient(90deg, #333, #333 5px, transparent 5px, transparent 10px) 2;. This creates a dashed border with 5px dashes and 5px gaps.

advanced.question: Explain the complete border-radius algorithm including the 'overlapping curves' adjustment.
advanced.answer: When two adjacent border-radius curves overlap (their radii sum exceeds the side length), the browser scales down the radii proportionally to prevent overlapping. The formula: f = min(side_length / (radius1 + radius2)) for each side. If f < 1, all radii are multiplied by f. For example, a 100px element with border-radius: 80px has overlapping curves, so the actual radius becomes 50px (f = 100/160 = 0.625, 80 * 0.625 = 50).

advanced.question: How does the browser render anti-aliased borders and what causes jagged edges on rounded corners?
advanced.answer: Border anti-aliasing smooths diagonal/curved edges by rendering partially transparent pixels at the boundary. On low-DPI screens, border curves can appear jagged or stair-stepped. The browser uses sub-pixel rendering techniques to mitigate this. Jaggedness is more noticeable with thinner borders and on low-resolution screens. Using slightly larger border-width or applying a subtle box-shadow with same color can help smooth edges.

advanced.question: What happens when border-radius is applied to elements with different display values (block, inline, table)?
advanced.answer: border-radius clips the element's background and border to the rounded shape. On block elements, this works normally. On inline elements, border-radius clips only the background within the inline box — but since inline elements can break across lines, each fragment gets its own clipping, possibly causing visual gaps at line breaks. On table elements with border-collapse: collapse, border-radius may be ignored or behave unexpectedly. On table elements without collapsed borders, border-radius works, but it clips the outer table edge only.

advanced.question: How does the border shorthand interact with the box-shadow property when creating compound border effects?
advanced.answer: box-shadow can be layered with borders to create complex edge effects. Multiple box-shadows with different spread values can simulate inner borders, outer borders, or glow effects without affecting layout: box-shadow: 0 0 0 3px blue, 0 0 0 6px red, creates two concentric 'borders' around an element. Unlike actual borders, these don't add to the element's box model dimensions — they overlay on top of margins.

advanced.question: How does the CSS specification define the rendering of 'double' border-style when the border-width is very thin?
advanced.answer: The 'double' border-style renders as two parallel lines with a gap between them. According to the spec, the two lines and the gap each equal one-third of the border-width. For border-width: 3px, each line and gap is 1px. For border-width: 2px (which isn't evenly divisible by 3), the browser rounds — typically making the lines thicker than the gap. For border-width: 1px, most browsers render a single line (unable to show two distinct lines at 1px).

advanced.question: How can you create a gradient border using background-clip and padding-box without border-image?
advanced.answer: Use a technique with two background layers and background-clip: .element { border: 3px solid transparent; background-clip: padding-box, border-box; background-origin: padding-box, border-box; background-image: linear-gradient(white, white), linear-gradient(45deg, red, blue); }. The first gradient (white) covers the padding/content area; the second gradient shows through the transparent border area where padding-box clips it away.

advanced.question: What are the differences in border rendering between box-sizing: content-box and border-box?
advanced.answer: With content-box, the border is added outside the specified width/height — it increases the element's total size. With border-box, the border is subtracted from the content area — the specified width includes the border, so content width = width - border-left - border-right. The visual rendering of the border itself is identical in both cases, but the layout calculations differ significantly.

advanced.question: How does the browser resolve conflicting border-colors when using transparent borders with adjacent elements?
advanced.answer: When using transparent borders (e.g., in CSS triangles or spacing techniques), the background of the element shows through the transparent border area. If the element has a gradient image background, the transparent border shows the gradient. For adjacent elements with transparent borders, they show their own backgrounds — there's no visual merging. The transparent value allows the element's own background to be visible where the border would otherwise cover it.

advanced.question: Explain the SVG border rendering model when border is applied to SVG elements like rect or circle.
advanced.answer: SVG elements use a different rendering model than HTML elements. The CSS border properties apply to SVG elements only if they are replaced elements (like <image>). For SVG shapes (<rect>, <circle>), you should use SVG-specific attributes (stroke, stroke-width) instead. Modern browsers do support CSS border properties on SVG root elements and SVG foreignObject but not on basic SVG shapes directly — those need SVG stroke properties.

advanced.question: How can border properties be animated with CSS transitions and what performance considerations apply?
advanced.answer: border-color can be animated (transitioned) efficiently as it only changes a color value. border-width can be animated but causes layout reflow because it changes element dimensions. border-radius can be animated efficiently as it's a composited property in most browsers. For performant border animations: prefer animating border-color and border-radius over border-width, use transform: scale() instead of animating border-width for hover effects, and use box-shadow for glow animations instead of border animations.
